title: SEGMENT 11 — Pre-existing immunity in humans: the seroprevalence problem

lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — Pre-existing immunity in humans: the seroprevalence problem
Now we have to confront the largest single barrier to adenoviral vector deployment in humans, and one of the central reasons the field moved in the directions it did over the last twenty years.
The problem is pre-existing immunity — the fact that most adult humans have already met most common adenoviral types in childhood and carry serum antibodies against them.
Let me put the numbers back on the table from Segment 2, because they're the spine of this segment.
For Ad5 specifically — the most commonly used vector backbone — anti-Ad5 neutralizing antibodies are detectable in roughly 40 to 70 percent of adults in the United States and Europe.
In sub-Saharan Africa, the prevalence runs higher, often over 90 percent.
For other common human adenoviral types — Ad2, Ad6, Ad26, Ad35 — the prevalence varies by type and by region, but it's never trivial.
What does pre-existing immunity actually do to a vector?
Several things, all bad.
One: it neutralizes the vector before it reaches target cells.
Anti-hexon antibodies bind the capsid, opsonize the particle, and target it for clearance by liver and spleen macrophages.
A patient with high anti-Ad5 titers can clear a vector dose dramatically faster than a patient with low titers.
The effective dose delivered to the intended tissue is correspondingly lower.
Two: it amplifies the acute inflammatory response.
Antibody-coated capsids activate complement more efficiently than uncoated capsids, drive more cytokine release from Fc-receptor-bearing immune cells, and worsen the infusion reaction.
Patients with high pre-existing immunity have worse acute side effects from adenoviral vectors.
Three: it reduces the efficacy of the encoded therapy.
Whatever payload the vector was supposed to deliver gets delivered at lower levels because fewer vector particles reach target cells.
For a gene therapy, this means less corrective protein.
For a vaccine, this means less expressed antigen and a weaker immune response to the antigen.
Four: it eliminates the possibility of redosing with the same vector.

Even if you sneak a first dose past pre-existing immunity, by the time you'd want to give a second dose — weeks later, for a vaccine boost, or months later, for a gene therapy top-up — the patient has now developed even higher antibody titers from their first exposure to your vector.
A second dose of the same vector gets neutralized even more efficiently than the first.
So adenoviral vectors are, in practice, one-shot tools unless you can switch to a different serotype for the boost.
There are several strategies to deal with the pre-existing immunity problem.
Strategy one: use rare human serotypes.
Instead of Ad5, use an adenovirus that most people have never been exposed to.
The most prominent example is Ad26, a relatively rare human adenoviral type with much lower seroprevalence in most populations — typically 10 to 30 percent in adult populations.
Ad26-based vectors have been developed for HIV, Ebola, Zika, and most famously for the Johnson and Johnson COVID-19 vaccine.
Using a rare serotype doesn't eliminate the problem, but it shifts the proportion of patients who can effectively receive the vector from a minority to a majority.
Strategy two: use animal adenoviruses, particularly chimpanzee adenoviruses.
Humans have never been infected with chimpanzee adenoviruses, so humans have no pre-existing immunity to them.
The ChAdOx1 vector — chimpanzee adenovirus Oxford 1 — is the backbone for the AstraZeneca-Oxford COVID-19 vaccine.
We'll spend the whole next segment on this strategy, because it's important enough to deserve its own treatment.
Strategy three: chemically modify the capsid.
You can coat the capsid surface with polyethylene glycol — PEGylation — to physically shield the antigenic surface from neutralizing antibodies.
PEGylated adenoviral vectors are partly protected from pre-existing antibodies, though the protection is incomplete and the chemistry adds production complexity.
PEGylation also slows clearance, which can be a benefit.
Strategy four: heterologous prime-boost.
Use one vector for the first dose and a different vector for the second dose.
The pre-existing immunity from dose one doesn't carry over to dose two if the two vectors are antigenically distinct.
The Sputnik V COVID-19 vaccine actually uses this strategy within a single course — the first dose is Ad26-based, the second dose is Ad5-based.
The two adenoviral types are antigenically different enough that the anti-Ad26 antibodies from dose one don't substantially neutralize the Ad5 in dose two.
The big idea here: adenoviral vector engineering is, in significant part, an arms race against pre-existing human immunity.
Every strategy I just listed is an attempt to dodge or outflank the neutralizing antibodies that adult humans carry around against common adenoviral types.

The field has been quite creative — rare serotypes, animal serotypes, capsid modifications, prime-boost regimens — but the fundamental problem is unsolvable in principle, because the same surface features that let you exploit the virus's tropism are the surface features that the immune system can learn to recognize.
You can change the surface, and the immune system will eventually learn the new surface too, given enough exposure.

The strategy that has worked best in practice — that allowed AstraZeneca to dose hundreds of millions of people with their COVID vaccine — is to switch to a serotype the human immune system has essentially never seen.
Chimpanzee adenovirus.
Let's spend a full segment on that.
